sections.headline: The Parable of the Farmer and the Soils

verses.text: That day, Jesus left the house and sat by the sea.
Large crowds gathered around him, so he got into a boat and sat down, while the crowd stood on the shore.
He spoke to them in parables: “A farmer went out to sow seed.”
As he sowed, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it.
Other seed fell on rocky ground, where it sprang up quickly, having little soil.
But when the sun rose, it was scorched, and since it had no roots, it withered.
Other seed fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up and choked it.
Yet other seed fell on good soil and produced a crop; some a hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.
Whoever has ears, let them listen.

sections.headline: The Purpose of Parables and Their Explanation

verses.text: The disciples came to him and asked, “Why do you speak to them in parables?”
He answered, “Because the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven have been given to you, but not to them.
For those who have, more will be given, and they will have abundance. But those who do not have, even what they have will be taken away.
This is why I speak to them in parables: Though hearing, they do not hear; seeing, they do not see or understand.
In them the prophecy of Isaiah is fulfilled: ‘You will hear and never understand; you will see and never perceive.’

For this people’s hearts have grown hard; they barely hear with their ears, and they have closed their eyes. Otherwise, they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn, and He would heal them.
But blessed are your eyes, for they see, and your ears, for they hear.
Truly I tell you, many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it.
Therefore, listen to the parable of the sower:
When someone hears the word of the kingdom but does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown in their heart. This is the seed along the path.
The seed on rocky ground is the one who hears the word and instantly receives it with joy,
but since it has no root, it lasts only a short time. When trouble or persecution comes because of the word, they quickly fall away.
The seed among thorns is the one who hears the word, but the worries of this age and the deceitfulness of wealth choke the word, making it unfruitful.
But the seed on good soil is the one who hears the word and understands it, bearing fruit and yielding a hundredfold, sixty, or thirty.”
He presented another parable to them: “The kingdom of heaven is like a man who sowed good seed in his field.
But while his slaves were sleeping, his enemy came and sowed darnel among the wheat and went away.
When the wheat sprouted and yielded grain, the darnel appeared too.
The slaves came to the master of the house and said, ‘Master, did you not sow good seed in your field? Where then did the darnel come from?’
He replied, ‘An enemy did this.’ The slaves asked, ‘Do you want us to gather them?’
‘No,’ he said, ‘lest you uproot the wheat with them.’
Let both grow together until the harvest. Then I will tell the reapers, ‘First gather the darnel and tie it into bundles to burn it, then gather the wheat into my barn.’”
He presented another parable to them: “The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed that a man took and sowed in his field.
It is the smallest of all the seeds, but when it grows, it surpasses the garden plants and becomes a tree, so that the birds of the air come and nest in its branches.”
He told another parable: “The kingdom of heaven is like yeast that a woman took and put into three measures of flour until the whole batch was fully leavened.”
Jesus spoke all these things to the crowds in parables. He said nothing to them without a parable,
to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet: ‘I will open my mouth in parables; I will declare things hidden since the foundation of the world.’
Then he left the crowds and went into the house. His disciples came to him and asked, “Explain to us the parable of the darnel in the field.”
He answered, “The one who sows the good seed is the Son of Man.
The field is the world, the good seed is the children of the kingdom, and the darnels are the children of the evil one.
The enemy who sowed them is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age, and the reapers are angels.
Just as the darnels are gathered and burned in the fire, so it will be at the end of the age.
The Son of Man will send out his angels and they will gather from his kingdom all causes of sin and the workers of lawlessness,
and throw them into the fiery furnace, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father. Whoever has ears, let them listen.”
“The kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field. When someone found it, they hid it again and, in their joy, sold all they had and bought that field.
Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a merchant seeking fine pearls.”
“When they found one of great value, they went away, sold all they had, and bought it.”
“Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a net let down into the sea, gathering fish of every kind.”
When it was full, they pulled it onto the shore, sat down, and collected the good fish into baskets, but the bad they threw away.
So it will be at the end of the age: the angels will separate the wicked from among the righteous.
And they will throw them into the fiery furnace, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Jesus said to them, “Have you understood all these things?” They replied, “Yes.”
He said to them, “Therefore, every scribe trained for the kingdom of heaven is like the master of a house who brings out of his storeroom both new and old things.”
When Jesus had finished these parables, he left that place.
Coming to his hometown, he taught them in their synagogue, and they were astonished, saying, “Where did this man get this wisdom and these miracles?
Is not this one the son of the carpenter? Is not his mother called Mary, and his brothers James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas?
And are not all his sisters with us? Where then did this man get all these things?”
And they were offended by him. But Jesus said to them, “A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown and in his own household.”
And he did not do many miracles there because of their unbelief.

footnotes.content: The saying “For whoever has, more will be given… but whoever does not have, even what they have will be taken away” is a spiritual proverb Jesus repeats throughout the Gospels (Matthew 25:29; Mark 4:25; Luke 8:18). It illustrates a divine principle of revelation: those who respond with faith and receptivity are given greater understanding, while those who resist lose even the truth they once held. In context, Jesus applies this to the parables—His disciples “have” because they seek, ask, and follow, so they are entrusted with the mysteries of the kingdom (v. 11). The crowds, however, remain passive or hardened, and thus truth is veiled. The Greek construction highlights this contrast of spiritual responsiveness. The word “have” does not refer to possessions, but to an open posture toward God's word. This principle echoes Proverbs 9:9, “Give instruction to a wise man, and he will be still wiser,” and is directly connected to Jesus' repeated warnings to listen with intent (Mark 4:24; Luke 8:18).
Farmers burned darnel (a poisonous wheat look-alike) outside the village to prevent reseeding. Jesus uses this known harvest practice to frame the final judgment.

The Greek word for “yeast” is zyme, which in many Jewish contexts symbolized corruption or impurity (Exodus 12:15; Matthew 16:6). However, Jesus here reverses the symbol: the yeast represents the quiet, permeating power of the kingdom of heaven. Just as a small amount of leaven transforms a large batch of dough from within, so the kingdom spreads invisibly and irreversibly until it fills the whole. The image emphasizes hidden influence rather than external force.

The word zyme refers to fermented dough used as a rising agent. In Scripture, leaven often symbolizes hidden influence—typically negative (such as sin or hypocrisy), but here it is used positively to illustrate the transforming power of the kingdom of heaven. The “3 measures of flour” would yield enough bread to feed more than 100 people, emphasizing the kingdom's overwhelming effect. Though it begins invisibly and in small form, its impact quietly permeates everything it touches.
“Fiery furnace” alludes to Danielic imagery of divine judgment (Daniel 3:6, 3:23).

The phrase “hid again” reflects common legal practice in the ancient world. According to 1st-century Jewish and Roman law, buried treasure did not automatically belong to the finder—it was tied to ownership of the land. By hiding the treasure again, the person ensures they can legally acquire it by purchasing the field. The action highlights urgent joy and wise intent: giving up everything for what is of immeasurable worth.

The Greek word traditionally translated “scribe” grammateus referred to someone trained in the Torah—an expert in Jewish law. By saying “becomes a disciple,” Jesus implies that even these respected teachers must undergo transformation and submit to the new reality of the kingdom. The image of bringing out “new and old” treasures points to their role in weaving together the wisdom of the Hebrew Scriptures with the fresh revelation found in Christ. The kingdom does not discard the old—it fulfills it.

The phrase “offended” translates the Greek skandalizō, from which we get the word “scandal.” It means to stumble over, to be repelled by, or to reject because of a perceived contradiction or unacceptability. The townspeople could not reconcile Jesus' divine wisdom and miracles with their familiarity with him. This paradox—divine glory clothed in local ordinariness—provoked contempt rather than faith.
